Industry Vibes

*DG:* Thanks for having us again, and yeah I come bearing tidings from New =

icial intelligence, but for one week in early December, it is. There=E2=80=

ferent name, and it is, I think, the largest AI conference in the United St=

trialized it is now. I=E2=80=99ve gone there a few years, and, you know, in=

 who were working on alchemy that no one else appreciated, and when I showe=
d up this time, there were three different simultaneous parties by three di=
fferent quant firms trying to attract the best and the brightest. Everyone =
should have a spouse that looks at them like the recruiters of these quant =
firms look at the passing array of math and machine learning wizards that g=
o through.

There were booths basically for every major tech company except OpenAI, whi=
ch I thought was sort of interesting, and as a random aside, there were act=
ually two distinct booths for Google. There was a DeepMind booth, and then =
there was a Google Research booth. You could sort of see Jeff Dean ( https:=
//research.google/people/jeffrey-dean/ ) literally standing in between both=
 of them not quite sure which one he belongs to, and you know what I though=
t was sort of interesting was just to talk to the different recruiters of t=
hese companies that attend these conferences in an attempt to recruit these=
 engineers, you really get a sense of what the market is like and the recru=
iters of course all talk to each other and learn interesting things.

As a random aside, it seemed like Nvidia really started paying top dollar f=
or machine learning talent, competing with the likes of OpenAI, which is no=
t something I=E2=80=99d expected, and you could also see the businesses tha=
t ultimately have a heroic mission, meaning that they=E2=80=99re working on=
 something like AGI, actually managed to get by by hiring fairly high quali=
ty people and paying them very little. It=E2=80=99s funny that the people I=
 think that struggle the most are either the very large established compani=
es like LG or I don=E2=80=99t know Sony that are not known as AI-forward =
=E2=80=94 they have the largest booths =E2=80=94 and all these financial fi=
rms. It really goes to show you at the end of the day, the fitness function=
 that these people work on is something a little bit spiritual, which I thi=
nk may tie in to all that drama we=E2=80=99ve been seeing a little bit at O=
penAI lately.

*Do you feel that=E2=80=99s still the case? Because you mentioned you had a=
ll these refugees six, seven years ago, and now as it=E2=80=99s transformed=
 and there are a huge number of opportunities and it=E2=80=99s become indus=
trialized to your point, is that vibe shifting at all or is this spiritual =
mission of AI still a real fundamental incentive for people in the field?*

*DG:* California has always been the birthplace of cults and religions =E2=
=80=94 the hippie movement started here, LSD, that whole thing =E2=80=94 an=
d that I think is evergreen, and to some extent, in the early days of finte=
ch, it was also somewhat of a spiritual movement. You=E2=80=99re right that=
 as it crested and became normie and companies went public, it became more =
of a mainstream thing.

I think we=E2=80=99re still in the 1994 equivalent of AI where it is very m=
uch a spiritual project, and AI is a little bit different from fintech or t=
he Internet in the sense that it=E2=80=99s a little that as you get closer =
to the horizon event it only becomes more intense, because obviously the pe=
nultimate goal with AI is to, if you believe them, summon a kind of god, an=
d so there is people =E2=80=94 it very much feels like walking through Los =
Alamos, what I imagine walking through Los Alamos must have felt like durin=
g the Manhattan Project, where there=E2=80=99s a real searching for a sense=
 of belonging and meaning and intellectual curiosity that=E2=80=99s the dri=
ving force behind all of this, and that creates, I think, distortions in th=
e market.

Paying salary to people is important so they feel like they=E2=80=99re not =
missing out on opportunity cost, but I don=E2=80=99t think it=E2=80=99s the=
 primary motivation of a lot of these people, which is also what drives a l=
ot of instability, I think, at some of these companies. They=E2=80=99re not=
 purely rationally motivated in some sense, it=E2=80=99s a point that Nat h=
as made is that we were probably only in the early innings of these kinds o=
f AI wars, just because the substrate of motivation in the field in Silicon=
 Valley has never been economical and it=E2=80=99s especially not economica=

*Is there a causal effect where you touched on something =E2=80=94 there ca=
n be a spiritual aspect, there can be a search for meaning, there can be a =

=80=99s not going to end, it=E2=80=99s going to be fine. Does that mean you=

=94 it=E2=80=99s hard to tease out, we=E2=80=99re psychoanalyzing the field=
 broadly, but as far as these personality traits and what folks actually pu=

*DG:* If I had to imagine the S curve of any technological field, I think a=
t the high G moment where you=E2=80=99re really ascending the S curve, you=
=E2=80=99re probably getting the least amount of pure capitalists and the m=
ost amount of people that are just fueled by different things. Now some of =

uff and Silicon Valley culturally goes through usually at this time every y=

Asymptotes and AI Religion

*What do you think, Nat?*

*NF:* I think the central question of AI right now is where is the asymptot=
e and how close are we to it? If it=E2=80=99s near and not too much higher,=

If we=E2=80=99re not near an asymptote if you know, my god, there is no asy=
mptote or at least none that we can perceive, then it=E2=80=99s hard to kno=
w what happens next, and we=E2=80=99re probably going to have a very differ=
ent civilization. I was looking at a chart recently on Twitter or on X ( ht=
tps://x.com/TrungTPhan/status/1735087248952185080?s=3D20 ) of how people me=

=E2=80=99s the Internet, that=E2=80=99s what it did, it=E2=80=99s a reorder=
ing our society at the fundamental genetic level in terms of how people mee=

=E2=80=99s going to be incredibly transformational.

*NF:* Right. Okay, so MoE stands for Mixture of Experts and the basic idea =
is that you take your model, and a neural network model has a bunch of laye=
rs in it and as you do the inference to compute the next token, the next wo=
rd, you pass through each of the layers in sequence and in a normal model, =
a so-called dense model, you just pass through the same layers every single=
 time.

In an MoE model, at each layer, you might have, say, eight copies of that l=
ayer, and each of those is called an expert, essentially. At each layer, yo=
u have a little router which makes a decision as to which expert to choose =
for this particular token that=E2=80=99s going to be generated and that rou=
ter will pick one or two of those experts. It=E2=80=99ll pass its feed forw=
ard to those experts, they=E2=80=99ll compute their result.

If it=E2=80=99s a top one MoE model, it=E2=80=99ll pick one of those and pa=
ss that forward to the next layer. If it=E2=80=99s top two, you can average=
 them and pass them through and what it means is that on a per-token basis,=
 you=E2=80=99re actually picking different sub-pieces of the network that y=
ou=E2=80=99re using.

*So it=E2=80=99s increasing parallelism.*

*DG:* It=E2=80=99s increasing expressivity, in a way.

*NF:* You can stupidly think about this as like, =E2=80=9COh, I=E2=80=99ve =

y layer, so if you have 50 layers in your network, you=E2=80=99re actually =

=E2=80=99s all priced in. So it=E2=80=99s a mistake I make throughout the c=
ourse of my life is I assume everyone=E2=80=99s having my thoughts and I as=
sume the market is efficient. Where in reality, the efficient market hypoth=
esis is possibly one of the greatest lies I=E2=80=99ve ever learned and has=
 cost me a lot of money.

Looking forward to the next year, I do think Nat=E2=80=99s point about agen=
ts is apt. We have this very basic issue in language models today where eve=
n as they make continual steps and they make mistakes, they don=E2=80=99t d=
o a great job of after making three mistakes being correct on the fourth tr=
y. It=E2=80=99s usually the case that if it starts making errors, it just g=
oes down the path of errors and then it starts making up API names that don=
=E2=80=99t exist and it just wants to get out of error hell. So coming up w=
ith better ways to basically search through the space of possible candidate=
 responses in the language models, high dimensionality embedding space, I t=
hink will be very important.

Maybe that=E2=80=99s a little bit of what we do with System 2. Maybe those =
metaphors aren=E2=80=99t apt, but basically selecting a breakthrough that s=
elects the next token much better than what we do today would be extremely =
impactful on a second derivative scale.

*It=E2=80=99s like slow thinking and fast thinking. The LLMs are fast think=
ing and you need a slow thinking layer on top that occasionally steps in an=
d resets things.*

*DG:* Exactly. We may look back on some of the techniques we use today such=
 as chain of thought where we have the language model tell itself it=E2=80=
=99s going to think step-by-step and we write out basically the slow down t=
hings, or we have a language model make a plan and then read the plan that =
it made. Those may be viewed as a horseless carriage, kerosene lamp prior t=
o the LED sort of thing. Yeah, there may be just a much deeper, simpler way=
 to do that. If I had to guess, maybe there=E2=80=99s just so many people w=
orking on it, that=E2=80=99s the big breakthrough of the year.

*Very good. Well, it=E2=80=99s been quite a year. It feels like every time =
you guys are on, have been gone quite frequently over the last 18 months, a=
nd yet every time we go way over because it feels like there=E2=80=99s so m=
uch to talk to and I have ten questions I didn=E2=80=99t even get to. It wa=
s good to see you guys, good to talk to you and I=E2=80=99m confident we=E2=
=80=99ll have plenty more to talk about soon.*

*DG:* Sounds great, looking forward to it. Thanks, Ben.
